original_abstract:
Background: Diabetic nephropathy (DN) remains one of the most severe complications of diabetes mellitus, necessitating the discovery of effective therapeutic interventions. While rhein has shown promise in treating DN, the precise molecular mechanisms underlying its efficacy remain unclear. This study investigated the protective role of rhein and its impact on the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway.

Methods: We utilized a high glucose (HG)-induced podocyte injury model and a streptozocin (STZ)-induced DN mouse model, both treated with rhein. Cell viability was assessed via CCK-8 assays, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels were quantified using flow cytometry. We employed RT-qPCR to measure the expression of Ras-related C3 botulinum toxin substrate 1 (Rac1), NADPH Oxidase 1 (NOX1), and β-catenin. Furthermore, immunofluorescence (IF) was used to characterize glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4), α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA), Nephrin, and Podocin. Renal morphology and tubulointerstitial fibrosis were evaluated through HE and Masson staining, while immunohistochemistry (IHC) and specialized biochemical kits were used to analyze protein expression and metabolic markers.

Results: Rhein treatment significantly attenuated HG-induced oxidative stress, iron accumulation (Fe2+), and the expression of ferroptosis markers (TFR1, ACSL4) and mesenchymal markers (α-SMA, Vimentin, Snail, and Desmin). We found that rhein effectively suppressed the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin axis. Genetic silencing of Rac1 mirrored the protective effects of rhein by reducing MDA, Fe2+, and downstream target expression. Conversely, Rac1 overexpression exacerbated podocyte injury by inhibiting SOD, GSH, and GPX4 synthesis while downregulating SLC7A11 and Nephrin. In vivo, lentiviral-mediated Rac1 overexpression significantly promoted ferroptosis and fibrotic markers, reversing the therapeutic benefits observed with rhein.

Conclusions: Our findings demonstrate that rhein attenuates diabetic nephropathy by inhibiting ferroptosis and the epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT). These protective effects are mediated through the regulation of the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling axis, highlighting rhein as a potential therapeutic agent for managing DN.
humanized_abstract:
Title: Rhein exerts anti-diabetic nephropathy effects by suppressing the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway Background: Diabetic nephropathy (DN) is one of the main microvascular complications of diabetes mellitus. Searching for effective treatment for DN is a topic of considerable research interest. Rhein has been reported to possess potential therapeutic effects for diabetic nephropathy. However, the molecular mechanism is still unclear. In this study, we explored rhein's anti-diabetic nephropathy effects and its impact on the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway.

To verify the impact of rhein on HG-induced podocyte injury and STZ-induced DN, the cell viability was evaluated by CCK-8 assay and the ROS production by flow cytometry. In addition, the relative expression levels of Rac1, NOX1 and β-catenin were measured by RT-qPCR. The expression of GPX4, α-SMA, Nephrin and Podocin was examined by IF. The renal pathological changes and the extent of tubulointerstitial fibrosis were detected by HE and Masson staining, and the expressions of protein and metabolic related molecules were detected by IHC and corresponding biochemical kits.

Background Rhein has been shown to possess remarkable protective effects against a range of diseases, but its influence on high glucose (HG) induced podocyte injury and the underlying molecular mechanism remains unclear. Methods & Findings Human podocytes were treated with HG in the presence or absence of rhein. Rhein treatment attenuated HG-induced oxidative stress, iron accumulation (Fe2+), and expression of ferroptosis and mesenchymal markers (TFR1, ACSL4, α-SMA, Vimentin, Snail, Desmin). Rhein was then found to inhibit the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway. Through constructing Rac1 siRNA vector, it was confirmed that the effects of rhein on MDA, Fe2+, and target gene expression were mimicked by silencing Rac1. Furthermore, by transfecting Rac1 over-expression vector, the effects of rhein on reducing SOD, GSH and GPX4, as well as down-regulating SLC7A11 and Nephrin, were confirmed. In vivo, lentiviral-mediated Rac1 overexpression abolished the beneficial effects of rhein on ferroptosis and fibrosis. Conclusion Rhein mitigates HG-induced ferroptosis of podocytes via inhibition of Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin pathway.

Conclusion: Rhein exerts anti-diabetic nephropathy effects through inhibition of ferroptosis and epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and through regulation of the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway.

Background: Diabetic nephropathy (DN) remains one of the most severe complications of diabetes mellitus, necessitating the discovery of effective therapeutic interventions. This study investigated the protective role of rhein and its impact on the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway.
